questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Water is the most abundant compound on the Earth’s surface. It is very important to life on Earth.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the electron diagram for a water molecule, showing electrons in the outermost shells only.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q1_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
Nearly 98% of the water on Earth is sea water, which is not fit for human consumption. The diagram below shows the set-up used in a simple distillation experiment for obtaining water from sea water.

[IMG:q1_img_0.jpg]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Outline the underlying principle of this simple distillation experiment.
children.answer:
Water boils at about  \( 100^{\circ} \) C, but the salts in sea water are non-volatile.
The steam formed condenses on the cold surface of the condenser / cool down to give liquid water.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Insoluble solid S was placed into the flask before heating. Why?
children.answer: To prevent bumping / to prevent frothing / splash / overflow due to overheating of water 1

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Explain, from molecular level, why the density of ice is lower than that of water.
sub_questions.answer:
The attraction between water molecules is predominantly hydrogen bond.
Hydrogen bond is directional. In ice, the water molecules have a tetrahedral arrangement / are packed in an open structure.
In liquid water, the water molecules have relative motion and this leads to the collapse of the open structure. The molecules become more closely packed. So liquid water has higher density than ice.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Both  \( BF_{3} \)  and  \( NH_{3} \)  exist as simple molecules.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
For each of these molecules, draw its three-dimensional structure.
\( BF_{3} \)     \( NH_{3} \)
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:q2_img_0.jpg]
(Accept other correct representations of the trigonal planar structure.)

[IMG:q2_img_1.jpg]
(Showing the lone pair is NOT a must.)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: For each of these molecules, explain whether or not it is polar.
sub_questions.answer:
\( BF_{3} \)  is a non-polar molecule. The three polar B–F bonds are symmetrically arranged on the same plane.
\( NH_{3} \)  is a polar molecule. The central atom has a lone pair in its outermost shell and thus the three polar N–H bonds are not on the same plane.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: \( BF_{3} \)  reacts with  \( NH_{3} \)  to give  \( F_{3}BNH_{3} \) . Describe the bond formation between  \( BF_{3} \)  and  \( NH_{3} \) .
sub_questions.answer:
In  \( BF_{3} \) , there are three bonding electron pairs / there is a vacant site in the outermost shell of the B atom.
By accepting the lone pair of electrons from the N atom of  \( NH_{3} \)  / forming dative bond with N, the B atom attains the stable electronic configuration of neon (a noble gas).

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text: Compound W contains carbon, hydrogen and oxygen only. The relative molecular mass of W is 88.0. Complete combustion of 1.32 g of W gives 2.64 g of carbon dioxide and 1.08 g of water.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
Deduce the molecular formula of W.
(Relative atomic masses: H = 1.0, C = 12.0, O = 16.0)
sub_questions.answer:
C H O
Atom ratio  \( \frac{2.64}{44} : 2 \times \frac{1.08}{18} : \frac{0.48}{16} \)
= 2 : 4 : 1
Empirical formula is  \( C_{2}H_{4}O \)
Molecular formula is  \( (C_{2}H_{4}O)_{n} \)
\( n \times (12 \times 2 + 1 \times 4 + 16 \times 1) = 88.0 \)
n = 2
Molecular formula of W is  \( C_{4}H_{8}O_{2} \)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Given that W has only one functional group, draw TWO possible structures of W.
sub_questions.answer:
Any two of the following structures. 1 mark for each structure.
\( CH_{3}CH_{2}CH_{2}COOH \)
\( (CH_{3})_{2}CHCOOH \)
\( H_{3}CCOOCH_{2}CH_{3} \)
\( CH_{3}CH_{2}COOCH_{3} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The structure of a dibasic acid with chemical formula  \( H_{2}C_{2}O_{4} \)  is shown below:
\(COOH\)
\( \mid\)
\(COOH\)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Give the systematic name of this dibasic acid.
sub_questions.answer: Ethanedioic acid

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: A student expected a 0.0500 mol dm \( ^{-3} \)  standard H \( _{2} \) C \( _{2} \) O \( _{4} \) (aq) to have a pH of 1.0. However, the pH of the solution, when measured with a calibrated pH meter, was found to be greater than 1. Explain this observation with the aid of a chemical equation.
sub_questions.answer:
\( H_{2}C_{2}O_{4}(aq) \rightleftharpoons C_{2}O_{4}^{2-}(aq) + 2H^{+}(aq) / H_{2}C_{2}O_{4}(aq) \rightleftharpoons HC_{2}O_{4}^{-}(aq) + H^{+}(aq) \)
\( H_{2}C_{2}O_{4} \)  is a weak acid. It undergoes incomplete ionisation in water. As  \( pH = -\log_{10}[H^{+}(aq)] \) , its pH is thus greater than 1.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Solid sodium hydroxide is available in school laboratories. However, standard NaOH(aq) CANNOT be directly prepared by weighing NaOH(s) and then dissolving it in water. Explain why.
sub_questions.answer:
NaOH(s) is deliquescent / absorbs water from the atmosphere.
Or, NaOH(s) reacts with  \( CO_{2}(g) \)  in the atmosphere.
So the mass of NaOH(s) cannot be accurately determined by weighing.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: In a titration experiment, 25.00 cm \( ^{3} \)  of a 0.0500 mol dm \( ^{-3} \)  standard H\( _{2} \)C\( _{2} \)O\( _{4} \)(aq) and a few drops of phenolphthalein indicator were placed in a conical flask. NaOH(aq) of unknown concentration was then added from a burette into the flask. 17.20 cm \( ^{3} \)  of the NaOH(aq) was required to reach the titration end point.

children.label: i
children.question_text: State the colour change at the titration end point.
children.answer: From colourless to pink

children.label: ii
children.question_text: From the titration results, calculate the concentration of the NaOH(aq), in mol dm \( ^{-3} \) .
children.answer:
concentration of NaOH(aq) 
\(  = \frac{0.05 \times 25 \times 2}{17.20}  \)
\(  = 0.145 \, \text{mol dm}^{-3}  \)

sub_questions.label: e
sub_questions.question_text: The following were considered as INAPPROPRIATE practices when carrying out the experiment in (d). For each of them, explain why it would lead to inaccurate titration results:

children.label: i
children.question_text: rinsing the conical flask with the standard  \( H_{2}C_{2}O_{4}(aq) \)  before transferring  \( 25.00\ cm^{3} \)  of the acid solution to it
children.answer: Rinsing the conical flask with  \( H_{2}C_{2}O_{4}(aq) \) : Some  \( H^{+}(aq) \)  ions remain in the flask, and more alkali (as revealed from the burette reading) than actually required is used to reach the titration end point.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: carrying out the titration with the filter funnel remained on top of the burette after using it to fill the burette with the NaOH(aq)
children.answer: NaOH(aq) clinging onto the stem of funnel may fall into the burette. The volume of alkali used (as revealed from the burette reading) is smaller than what is expected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The following experiment was carried out to determine the enthalpy change of solution of ammonium nitrate:

\( \mathrm{NH_{4}NO_{3}(s)\xrightarrow{H_{2}O(l)}NH_{4}NO_{3}(aq)} \)

A certain volume of water was placed in an expanded polystyrene cup. The temperature of the water in the cup was measured with a thermometer at half-minute intervals. Right at the third minute, 2.0 g of  \( NH_{4}NO_{3}(s) \)  was added to the cup. The solution in the cup was then stirred thoroughly and its temperature was measured for an additional 7 minutes.
The recordings of temperature are shown in the graph below:

[IMG:q5_img_0.jpg]

children.label: i
children.question_text: From the graph, estimate the greatest temperature drop of the solution in the cup.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: The mass of the  \( NH_{4}NO_{3}(aq) \)  obtained was found to be 21.8 g. Calculate the enthalpy change of solution of ammonium nitrate, in kJ mol \( ^{-1} \) , under the experimental conditions. (Assume that the heat capacity of the expanded polystyrene cup is negligible, and the specific heat capacity of the  \( NH_{4}NO_{3}(aq) \)  obtained is  \( 4.3\ J\ g^{-1}\ K^{-1} \) .)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest ONE way of keeping \(NH_{4}NO_{3}(s)\) dry during storage.
sub_questions.answer: Place \(NH_{4}NO_{3}(s)\) in a desiccator / sealed container containing drying agent (anhydrous \(CaCl_{2}(s)\), \(CaO(s)\), \(MgSO_{4}(s)\), etc.) / silica gel

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text: Briefly describe how polypropene can be produced from naphtha.
questions.answer:
• Cracking of naphtha gives a mixture of hydrocarbons which include propene.
• Fractional distillation of the gaseous products can separate propene from other hydrocarbons.
• Polymerisation of propene at elevated temperatures / > 45°C / high pressure / > 5 atm / in the presence of a suitable catalyst / Ziegler-Natta catalyst gives polypropene.
• Communication mark

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text: Thermit reactions broadly refer to exothermic oxidation-reduction reactions between a metal powder and a metal oxide. One example is the reaction of finely divided iron(III) oxide with aluminium powder. This reaction results in a very high temperature, and is commonly used in the welding of rail tracks for trains. At this very high temperature, the molten iron formed joins the rail tracks together.

children.label: i
children.question_text:
Complete and balance the chemical equation for the following thermite reaction.

\(\_\_\_ \mathrm{Fe}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{s}) + \_\_\_ \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \)
children.answer: \( \mathrm{Fe}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{s}) + 2\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow 2\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{l}) + \mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{s}) \)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Sketch a labelled enthalpy level diagram for this reaction.
children.answer: [IMG:q7_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Copper powder CANNOT be used to replace aluminium powder in carrying out the thermite reaction with iron(III) oxide. Explain why.
sub_questions.answer: Copper is less reactive than iron / copper has a lower affinity for oxygen than iron. / Copper is a weaker reducing agent than iron. / Copper is lower than iron in the chemical reactivity series. So Cu(s) cannot reduce  \( Fe_{2}O_{3}(s) \) .

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: The extraction of iron from its ores also involves the reduction of iron oxides.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Suggest why aluminium is NOT used as the reducing agent in iron extraction.
children.answer: Aluminium is more expensive than iron.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest ONE reducing agent commonly used in iron extraction.
children.answer: coke / carbon / charcoal / carbon monoxide / CO

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Both caesium (Cs) and sodium (Na) are elements in Group I of the Periodic Table. Caesium reacts with chlorine to form caesium chloride.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Write the chemical equation for the reaction of caesium with chlorine.
sub_questions.answer: \( 2Cs + Cl_{2} \rightarrow 2CsCl \)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Solid caesium chloride has a giant ionic structure.
sub_questions.answer:
(i)

• Chloride ion
• Caesium ion

children.label: i
children.question_text: Draw a diagram to show the structure of caesium chloride.
children.answer: [IMG:q8_img_0.jpg]

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain why solid caesium chloride is brittle.
children.answer:
In CsCl, ions are strongly held by ionic bond.
Relative movement of the ions can bring ions of the same charge close to each other, and will result in repulsion. So CsCl(s) is brittle.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Predict, with ONE reason, whether sodium or caesium is more reactive towards chlorine.
sub_questions.answer: Cs(s) is more reactive than Na(s). The reactivity of Group 1 metals increases down the group. / The electron in the outermost shell of Cs is weakly bounded by the nucleus as compared with that of Na.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
The diagram below shows the set-up used in an investigation on the electrolysis of concentrated potassium iodide solution:

[IMG:q9_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: State and explain the expected observation around carbon electrode A during the electrolysis.
sub_questions.answer:
Brown colour is observed.
Due to the high concentration of KI in the solution, I\( ^{-} \)  ions are preferentially discharged to give I\( _{2} \)  which dissolves in KI(aq) to give brown I\( _{3} \)\( ^{-} \)  ions.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: The solution near carbon electrode B gradually turned blue.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Explain this observation.
children.answer:
\( H^{+} \)  ions is discharged and reduced to  \( H_{2} \)  at electrode B.
The amount of  \( OH^{-} \)  ions increases at electrode B as  \( H^{+} \)  is being consumed. So universal indicator turns blue under alkaline conditions.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Would there be any change in observation if carbon electrode B is replaced by a copper electrode in the investigation? Explain.
children.answer:
Accept both 'yes' and 'no' answers.
'No': B is the negative electrode. Copper will not lose electrons to give  \( Cu^{2+} \)  at the negative electrode / Copper cannot undergo reduction at the negative electrode.
'Yes': Copper and carbon have different electrical conductivity. Therefore the solution near electrode B turns blue more quickly. / The current in the external circuit changes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
The diagram below shows the structure of a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell using concentrated potassium hydroxide solution as the electrolyte.

[IMG:q10_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
An oxygen cylinder can be used to provide oxygen for the above fuel cell. From the hazard warning labels shown below, circle the label that should be displayed on the oxygen cylinder.

[IMG:q10_img_1.jpg]
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q10_1.jpg]

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
Write the half equation for the change occurring at each of the following electrodes when this fuel cell is producing a current.

electrode D

electrode E
sub_questions.answer:
electrode D:  \( H_{2}(g) + 2OH^{-}(aq) \rightarrow 2H_{2}O(l) + 2e^{-} \)
electrode E:  \( O_{2}(g) + 2H_{2}O(l) + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}(aq) \)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
Some people have the view that cars powered by hydrogen-oxygen fuel cells are more environmentally friendly than those powered by petrol.

Comment on this view from each of the following aspects:

children.label: i
children.question_text: source of fuel
children.answer:
Accept both ‘agree’ and ‘disagree’ answers.
Agree: The hydrogen gas can be obtained from renewable source (with one proper example)
Disagree: The hydrogen gas used is produced from fossil fuel such as steam reforming of natural gas. / Electrical energy is consumed in the production of hydrogen from water.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: the car emissions
children.answer: Agree: Only water is produced from the hydrogen-oxygen fuel cells. Or: No  \( CO_{2} \)  /  \( SO_{2} \)  /  \( NO_{x} \)  / CO / unburnt hydrocarbon in the exhaust.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Safety airbags are important devices installed in vehicles. During a serious car crash, the chemicals in the airbag immediately react to release a large amount of gas. An airbag hence inflates instantly, protecting the passenger. The main chemicals in safety airbags are sodium azide  \( \left(\mathrm{NaN}_{3}\right) \)  and potassium nitrate  \( \left(\mathrm{KNO}_{3}\right) \) . The equations below show the reactions involved when an airbag is inflated:

\( 2\mathrm{NaN}_{3}(\mathrm{s})\rightarrow2\mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+3\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \)
\( 10\mathrm{Na(s)}+2\mathrm{KNO_{3}(s)}\rightarrow\mathrm{K_{2}O(s)}+5\mathrm{Na_{2}O(s)}+\mathrm{N_{2}(g)} \)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why the  \( NaN_{3}(s) \)  and  \( KNO_{3}(s) \)  used in the airbags are in the form of fine powder.
sub_questions.answer: The airbag has to be inflated instantly when a car crash occurs. Fine powder can greatly increase the reaction rate by providing a large surface area for a reaction involving solid reactants.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.answer:
No. of mole of  \( N_{2} \)  produced from the decomposition of  \( NaN_{3} \)  (reaction 1)
\(  = \frac{100}{65} \times \frac{3}{2} = 2.31  \)
No. of mole of  \( N_{2} \)  produced from the reaction of Na with  \( KNO_{3} \)  (reaction 2):
No. of mole of Na produced =  \( \frac{100}{65} = 1.54 \)
(No. of mole of  \( KNO_{3} = \frac{200}{101.1} = 1.98 \)
Since 5 mol of Na react with 1 mol of  \( KNO_{3} \) ,  \( KNO_{3} \)  is in excess.)
No. of mole of  \( N_{2} \)  produced from reaction 2 =  \( \frac{100}{65} \times \frac{1}{10} = 0.154 \)
Volume of gas produced =  \( (2.31 + 0.154) \times 24 = 59.1 \, dm^{3} \)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: The main function of  \( NaN_{3}(s) \)  is to produce  \( N_{2}(g) \)  for inflating the airbags. Suggest why it is necessary to include  \( KNO_{3}(s) \)  in the airbags.
sub_questions.answer: \( KNO_{3} \)  is added to react with sodium which is highly reactive / corrosive / flammable / strongly reducing.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text:
Sodium azide is a toxic chemical. Thus any  \( NaN_{3} \)  waste remained during the manufacture of safety airbags needs special treatment before disposal. The treatment involves first dissolving  \( NaN_{3} \)  in water, and then reacting the solution formed with excess nitrous acid,  \( HNO_{2}(aq) \) . The graph below shows the variation of the concentration of  \( NaN_{3}(aq) \)  in the reaction mixture with time in one such process:

[IMG:q11_img_0.jpg]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Calculate the average rate of consumption of  \( \mathrm{NaN}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \)  in the first 10 seconds.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest how the instantaneous rate of consumption of  \( \mathrm{NaN}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \)  at the 10th second can be determined from the graph.
children.answer: Determine the slope of the tangent of curve at t = 10 s.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
At  \( 250^{\circ} \)C, the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the following reaction is  \( 25 \, mol^{-1} \, dm^{3} \) .

\(  PCl_{3}(g) + Cl_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons PCl_{5}(g) \)

A 10.0 dm \( ^{3} \)  sealed container, which is maintained at 250°C, initially contains 0.50 mol of PCl\( _{3} \) (g), 0.20 mol of Cl\( _{2} \) (g) and 0.40 mol of PCl\( _{5} \) (g).
questions.answer:
(a)
Reaction quotient =  \( \frac{(0.04)}{(0.05)(0.02)} \)  mol \( ^{-1} \)  dm \( ^{3} \)
= 40 mol \( ^{-1} \)  dm \( ^{3} \)
As reaction quotient >  \( K_{e} \) , so backward reaction rate is greater than the forward reaction rate.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: For this system under the initial conditions, calculate its reaction quotient. Predict and explain, under the initial conditions, whether the forward reaction rate or the backward reaction rate would be greater.
sub_questions.answer:
Reaction quotient = \( \frac{(0.04)}{(0.05)(0.02)}\ mol^{-1}\ dm^{3}\)
\( = 40\ mol^{-1}\ dm^{3}\)
As reaction quotient > \( K_{c} \), so backward reaction rate is greater than the forward reaction rate.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Calculate the concentration of  \( Cl_{2}(g) \)  when the system attains equilibrium at  \( 250^{\circ}C \) .
sub_questions.answer:
At equilibrium, the concentrations are:
\(  \left[ \mathrm{PCl}_{5}(\mathrm{~g}) \right] = (0.04 - y) \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{dm}^{-3}  \)
\(  \left[ \mathrm{PCl}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \right] = (0.05 + y) \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{dm}^{-3}  \)
\(  \left[ \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \right] = (0.02 + y) \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{dm}^{-3}  \)
\(  \frac{(0.04 - y)}{(0.05 + y)(0.02 + y)} = 25  \)
\(  y = 0.0052  \)
At equilibrium,  \(  \left[ \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \right]  \)  =  \( (0.02 + 0.0052) \)  mol dm \( ^{-3} \)  = 0.0252 mol dm \( ^{-3} \)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
0.10 mol of  \( Cl_{2}(g) \)  is added to the equilibrium mixture in (b). Sketch, in the graph below, the variation of the concentration of  \( Cl_{2}(g) \)  with time until a new equilibrium is attained. (Assume that the temperature of the system remains at  \( 250^{\circ}C \)  throughout the whole process.)

[IMG:q12_img_0.jpg]
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q12_b_0.jpg]

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 12: Patterns in the Chemical World
questions.question_text: Lithium, beryllium, carbon (graphite) and nitrogen are elements of the second period of the Periodic Table. Arrange them in increasing order of melting point, and explain the order in terms of structure and bonding.
questions.answer:
• Nitrogen < Lithium < Beryllium < Carbon (graphite)
• N₂ has the lowest melting point as it has a simple molecular structure, only weak van der Waals' forces / intermolecular forces need to be overcome in melting.
Both Li and Be have metallic structure, metallic bond in Li is weaker than that in Be. So Li < Be in melting points.
• C has the highest melting point as it has a giant covalent structure, large amount of energy is needed to break millions of strong covalent bonds between atoms in melting.
• Communication mark

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
An unsaturated fat F is a component of a vegetable oil. The structure of F is shown below:

[IMG:q14_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: State the reagents needed for converting F to a saturated fat.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Vegetable oils can be used to make soap.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Write the chemical equation involved for the formation of soap from F.
children.answer: [IMG:q14_img_1.jpg]

children.label: ii
children.question_text: In the presence of an acid, the soap formed in (i) can react with methanol to give compound G, which can be used as a biodiesel. Draw the structure of G.
children.answer: [IMG:q14_img_2.jpg]

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: With reference to their relative molecular masses and physical properties, explain why G can be used as a fuel for cars, but F cannot.
sub_questions.answer:
G has a smaller relative molecular mass than F, so G can be vaporised more easily than F. / 
G has a smaller relative molecular mass than F, so G has a lower boiling point than F. / 
G has a smaller relative molecular mass than F, and so the molecular size of G is smaller than that of F. The intermolecular attraction / van der Waals' forces between G are weaker than that between F, G can be vaporised more easily than F.
G burns more completely than F.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Consider the conversions of organic compounds shown below:
\(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2COCH_2CH_3} \quad (X) \xrightarrow{\text{reducing agent}} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH(OH)CH_2CH_3} \quad (Y) \xrightarrow{\text{dehydrating agent}} \text{a mixture of alkenes} \quad (Z)\)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest a chemical test to distinguish between X and Y.
sub_questions.answer:
Correct chemical reagent
Correct observations with comparison between the tests on X and Y

Possible chemical tests and the corresponding observations:
\( Cr_{2}O_{7}^{2-}/H^{+} \)  Observations: X – no change; Y – from orange to green
\( MnO_{4}^{-}/H^{+} \)  Observations: X – no change; Y – from purple to colourless
\( MnO_{4}^{-}/OH^{-} \)  Observations: X – no change; Y – brown precipitate formed
2,4-DNP Observations: X – formation of orange precipitate.; Y – no change
\( CH_{3}COOH/H^{+}/heat \)  Observations: X – no change; Y – fruity smell substance formed
\( (2,4-DNP = 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine) \)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest what reagent R might be.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: The mixture Z contains two alkenes with the same structural formula. Draw the respective structures of these two alkenes, and state their isomeric relationship.
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:q15_img_0.jpg]

Geometrical isomerism / cis/trans-isomerism

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: The alkenes in (c) can react with HCl to form an optically active chloroalkane. Write the structural formula of this chloroalkane.
sub_questions.answer: \( CH_3CHClCH_2CH_2CH_3 \)
